item.title: Leaked financial docs show OpenAI is losing billions of dollars a year
item.description: Audited accounting shows growing revenues being dwarfed by R&D, other expenses.

item.title: I wanted Bear Blog, but for my photos
item.description:
I love Bear.
It is one of the few places on the web that still feels calm, personal, and human. You write something, publish it, and it exists as a simple...

item.title: You Got Faster. Your Company Didn’t.
item.description: AI made you faster. But you’re not more productive; you’re outsourcing the slow part to everyone else

item.title: Microsoft's Secret 90s Weapon That Made Windows Fast
item.description:
Ever wonder how Windows managed to run so smoothly on the hardware of the 1990s? The answer lies in a highly classified internal Microsoft project codenamed "Microsoft LEGO".
Before modern compilers did all the heavy lifting, Microsoft engineers relied on a secret weapon to squeeze every last drop of performance out of your PC: the Basic Block Tool (BBT).
In this video, we are diving deep into the golden era of Windows development to uncover exactly how this hidden optimizer worked its magic. We'll break down the computer science behind it—explaining how BBT analyzed "basic blocks" of machine code (uninterrupted runs of instructions) and physically reorganized compiled binaries to maximize CPU cache efficiency and eliminate bottlenecks.
Whether you're a software engineer, a retro-tech enthusiast, or just love a good piece of hidden computer history, you won't want to miss this look under the hood of 90s-era Windows!
👇 What you'll learn in this video:
0:00 - The 90s PC Performance Problem 1:45 - What exactly is a "Basic Block"? 4:10 - The Secret Weapon: How Microsoft BBT was born 7:30 - How BBT physically rearranged binaries for speed 11:15 - Why it was kept a secret for so long 14:00 - Does this tech still exist today?
🔔 If you love deep dives into tech history and software engineering secrets, make sure to hit SUBSCRIBE and turn on notifications!
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.title: Announcing the early beta of Mathesar Cloud
item.description: Mathesar Cloud is now in early beta, with a limited free plan for using Mathesar without installing software or managing servers.

item.title: You (probably) don't need tls_insecure_skip_verify
item.description: There is a better way to proxy to an upstream HTTPS server

item.title: I got phished!
item.description: The hotel I booked my stay at got hacked and I got tricked into inputting my credit card details in a phishing attempt.

item.title: The Limits of Altruism
item.description:
I will try to explain why it is counter productive to design some systems fully basing their operations & maintenance on altruism.
I reference my personal frustrations & observations on certain open source projects.
item.author: (Asindu Drileba)

item.title: You don't know XPT files — KoliStat
item.description: A friendly tour of the SAS Transport (XPT) format: everything you never wanted to know about it, and how to decode its IBM hexadecimal floats.

item.description: A quick way to help with debugging plumbing issues in JAX training loops.

item.title: What does software development look like when agents write 100% of the code?
item.description: 2026 has been an inflection point for agentic coding. In just two years the capabilities of models and harnesses went from a toy autocomplete to being powerf...

item.title: Only 16 percent of Americans think AI will have a positive impact on society, a new study shows | TechCrunch
item.description: Although Wall Street loves AI, every day Americans are significantly less optimistic about the industry, a new report from Pew Research shows.

item.title: Volkswagen App - GrapheneOS Discussion Forum
item.description: GrapheneOS discussion forum

item.title: Learn about Lore: next-generation open source version control
item.description: Maintained by Epic Games, Lore is designed for unprecedented scalability of both data and teams. It’s optimized for projects that combine code with large binary assets.

item.title: Messi
Future of the Web 2026: AI Brand Visibility Research | WordPress VIP
item.description: Original research from 2,000 decision-makers and consumers on AI brand visibility, content trust, and what brands need to do as the web feels less human. 74% say the internet feels less human than it did 10 years ago.

item.title: Want your images back? Sure... That'll be $5!
item.description: We're in the era of trillion-dollar companies, but that doesn't mean you should leave $5 on the table!

item.title: Bubbles
item.description: Independent blog posts, ranked by the community. Good stuff bubbles up. The rest pops.

item.title: humiliating iis servers for fun and jail time
item.description: A friend of mine once told me: If you ever spot an IIS blue screen, don’t stop there; there must be something.
